title: Roster Management: Rename & Delete Schools and Players
summary: Studio admins can now rename or delete any school or player from their roster, with all album tags, photo tags, and customer watchlists updating automatically.
content:
A new Roster page (accessible from the sidebar under Studio → Roster) gives studio admins full control over the schools and players in their roster. Previously, schools and players were added automatically during album uploads and photo tagging, but there was no way to correct a misspelled name or remove an entry that was added by mistake.

Renaming a school updates every album's school tags in one operation. If "Lincoln High" was entered as "Lncoln High" across 40 albums, fixing the typo in the Roster page corrects all 40 albums instantly. The same rename also updates any customer watchlists — customers who were following the misspelled school name will automatically follow the corrected one.

Renaming a player cascades to every photo tagged with that player name. The player_names and player_numbers fields on photos are updated together so the pairing stays intact — renaming "Jon Smith" to "John Smith" on a player with number 77 updates all tagged photos while keeping the #77 association correct. Player watchlists and face recognition signatures are updated in the same operation.

Deleting a school removes it from the roster and strips it from all album tags. If the school had customers watching it, those watchlist entries are removed as well. Deleting a player removes it from the roster, removes the tag from all photos (including adjusting the corresponding number from the paired list), deletes watchlist subscriptions, and deletes any stored face recognition data for that player.

The Roster page shows how many albums or photos reference each entry and how many customers are watching it before you make any change. The delete confirmation dialog lists exactly what will be affected so there are no surprises.

title: Release Notes & Studio Announcements
summary: A new What's New page lets studio admins see detailed feature announcements. Super admins manage and publish release notes, and can email selected announcements to all studio admins at once.
content:
Studio admins now have a What's New page (sidebar → What's New) that shows a timeline of published feature announcements. Each entry includes a version date, a plain-English summary, and a full detailed description of the feature — written to explain not just what changed but how to use it and why it matters for your studio's workflow.

Super admins have a dedicated Release Notes management page (Super Admin → Release Notes) where they can create, edit, and publish notes. Notes can be saved as drafts before publishing so you can prepare announcements in advance. Each note has a title, a version date (displayed as a readable date like "June 24, 2026"), a short summary shown in the timeline preview, and a full detailed content body.

Release notes are generated automatically from git commit history every time the server starts. The system reads all commits since the last recorded note date, groups them by day, and writes new entries to a static release-notes.json file in the repository. Because the notes are stored in the repo and deployed with the code, no AI API key or git access is required on the production server — the server simply reads the file and syncs new entries into the database on startup.

For announcements you want to distribute by email, super admins can select any combination of release notes using checkboxes and click Email to Studios. A styled HTML email is sent BCC to all studio admin accounts, with each selected note appearing as a full section — title, date badge, summary, and complete content. This lets you choose exactly which features are important enough to proactively notify studios about, rather than emailing every minor update.

title: Saved Photos & Customer Favorites
summary: Customers can now save photos to a personal favorites list while browsing an album, filter to show only saved photos, and optionally provide an email address to retrieve their list later.
content:
Every photo in a customer-facing album now has a heart button. Tapping it saves or unsaves the photo to a session-based favorites list stored locally. Favorites persist across page reloads within the same browser session so customers can build a list as they browse without needing an account.

A Favorites filter button appears above the photo grid once at least one photo has been saved. Clicking it narrows the grid to show only saved photos, making it easy to review a shortlist before deciding what to order. The button label updates in real time to show how many photos are saved.

Customers can optionally enter an email address to associate with their favorites list. This lets them retrieve the same list later on a different device or share it with someone else (a parent, coach, or team administrator) by sending a link. The email is captured in the database alongside the session token so studios can see which emails are actively engaging with their albums.

Studios see favorites activity on the studio dashboard under a new Favorites section: total favorites saved, favorites in the last 7 and 30 days, unique sessions, and email captures. A conversion metric shows what percentage of favorited photos were eventually ordered — a useful signal for how well the favorites feature is driving purchases. The top 10 most-favorited photos, top albums by favorites activity, and top players with favorited photos are shown in ranked tables.

title: Album Share Links & Referral Tracking
summary: Studios can create trackable share links for any album. Each link has a unique code, an optional label, and records how many visits and orders it generated.
content:
The album settings panel in the admin Albums page now includes a Share Links section. You can create as many share links as you want for each album — for example, one link for a team's social media post, another for an email to parents, and a third for a coach's newsletter. Each link gets a short unique code appended to the album URL as a ?ref= parameter.

Every time someone visits the album through a share link, the visit is recorded. If that visitor places an order, the order is attributed to the referral code. This gives you a clear picture of which distribution channel is actually driving purchases: you can see at a glance that the Instagram post drove 312 visits and 18 orders while the email newsletter drove 89 visits and 41 orders, and adjust your marketing accordingly.

Each share code can be given a label ("Facebook Post", "Parent Email", "Team Group Chat") so you remember what it was for. The studio dashboard shows a Share Links section with summary stats — total codes created, total visits, total orders attributed to referrals, and recent activity over the last 7 and 30 days. Ranked tables show the top albums by referral traffic and the top individual codes by visits and orders.

Share links are permanent for the life of the album. You can create new codes at any time without invalidating old ones, so every historical referral remains tracked.

title: Per-Album Price Overrides
summary: Studios can now set custom product prices on a per-album basis, overriding the studio's default price list for specific albums or events.
content:
In the admin Albums page, each album's settings panel now includes a Price Overrides section. This lets you set a different price for any product size on that specific album without changing your studio-wide price list.

Common use cases include pricing a championship event differently from a regular season shoot, offering a discounted rate for a school you have a long-term relationship with, or testing higher prices for a premium event with limited attendance. Price overrides apply only to orders placed from that album — all other albums continue using your standard prices.

Overrides are set per product size. You can override the price for an 8x10 print in one album while leaving the 5x7 and wallet sizes at their standard prices. Any product size that doesn't have an explicit override falls back to the studio price list automatically, so you only need to enter the prices you want to change.

When a customer adds items to their cart from an overridden album, the cart resolves the override price transparently. Order receipts and admin order views show the price the customer actually paid, which reflects the override.

title: Package & Share Link Analytics on Studio Dashboard
summary: The studio dashboard now includes dedicated analytics sections for Package Sales and Share Links, showing adoption rates, revenue breakdowns, and top performers.
content:
Two new analytics sections have been added to the studio dashboard, joining the existing Favorites section introduced in the same release.

The Package Sales section shows how many orders included at least one pre-built package and what percentage of your total orders that represents (the adoption rate). It breaks down revenue into package revenue versus individual item revenue so you can see how much of your sales are coming from bundled offers versus à la carte ordering. A ranked table shows your top 10 packages by order count, total units sold, and total revenue, letting you see at a glance which packages your customers are choosing most often.

The Share Links section shows aggregate referral performance across all your albums: total share codes created, total visits from referral links, total orders attributed to referrals, and activity in the last 7 and 30 days. The overall conversion rate shows what percentage of referral visits result in an order. Two ranked tables drill down into the detail — top albums by referral traffic, and top individual share codes by visits and orders — so you can identify your most effective distribution channels and the albums that benefit most from active sharing.

Both sections appear only when the relevant data exists for your studio, so the dashboard remains uncluttered if you haven't created packages or share links yet.

title: WHCC Live Cost Lookup for Pricing
summary: Super admins can now fetch the real-time WHCC lab cost for any product variant directly from the pricing interface, without placing a test order.
content:
When building or reviewing a studio price list, super admins can now click a Fetch WHCC Cost button on any product variant row. This queries the WHCC API in real time and populates the base cost field with the actual current lab cost for that specific product and attribute combination.

Previously, setting accurate base costs required either manually referencing the WHCC catalog, placing test orders, or estimating based on product type. Any of those approaches could result in pricing that didn't account for the real lab cost, which affects profit margin calculations. The live lookup removes that guesswork entirely.

The lookup uses the same WHCC OrderImport endpoint that processes real orders, but in a read-only mode that retrieves costs without submitting anything. It resolves the product UID, node IDs, and attribute UIDs exactly as a real order would, so the cost returned is what WHCC would actually charge for that item. After fetching, the base cost is filled in automatically and saved to the variant row — you can then set your retail price with a clear picture of your margin.

This feature is available only to super admins in the Studio Price Lists section of the Super Admin tools. It requires valid WHCC API credentials to be configured in the server environment.
